elements.sent-2411-001: Mangroves play a crucial role in maintaining coastal ecosystem health and protecting biodiversity.
elements.sent-2411-002: Therefore, continuous mapping of mangroves is essential for understanding their dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-003: Earth observation imagery typically provides a cost-effective way to monitor mangrove dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, there is a lack of regional studies on mangrove areas in the UAE.
elements.sent-2411-005: This study utilizes the UNet++ deep learning model combined with Sentinel-2 multispectral data and manually annotated labels to monitor the spatiotemporal dynamics of densely distributed mangroves (coverage greater than 70\%) in the UAE from 2017 to 2024, achieving an mIoU of 87.8\% on the validation set.
elements.sent-2411-006: Results show that the total mangrove area in the UAE in 2024 was approximately 9,142.21 hectares, an increase of 2,061.33 hectares compared to 2017, with carbon sequestration increasing by approximately 194,383.42 tons.
elements.sent-2411-007: Abu Dhabi has the largest mangrove area and plays a dominant role in the UAE's mangrove growth, increasing by 1,855.6 hectares between 2017-2024, while other emirates have also contributed to mangrove expansion through stable and sustainable growth in mangrove areas.
elements.sent-2411-008: This comprehensive growth pattern reflects the collective efforts of all emirates in mangrove restoration.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, mangroves around the world are facing serious degradation and loss.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is estimated that the global mangrove area has decreased by 25\%-50\% since 1980\citep{Spalding2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: As one of the important countries with mangrove distribution on the Arabian Peninsula, the changes in the mangrove ecosystem in the UAE have attracted much attention.
elements.sent-2411-012: In recent years, with the development of remote sensing and geographic information system technology, research on the changes in the area of mangroves in the UAE has made some progress.
elements.sent-2411-013: On the one hand, urbanization and coastal development have led to the destruction of some mangroves\citep{Elmahdy2020,AlAbdulrazzak2019 ; on the other hand, the protection and restoration measures taken by the government have increased the area of mangroves in some areas\citep{Elmahdy2020,Almahasheer2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Al Habshi et al .\citep{AlHabshi2011 used remote sensing data to analyze the distribution of mangroves in the UAE and found that mangroves are mainly distributed in coastal areas of emirates such as Abu Dhabi, Dubai and Sharjah.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Moore et al. \citep{Moore2014 studied the distribution, pore water chemistry and community characteristics of mangroves in the UAE and found that the area of mangroves is about $41~\text{km ^2$ .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Alsumaiti et al. \citep{Alsumaiti2020 used Landsat satellite images to analyze the changes in mangroves on the eastern coast of Abu Dhabi from 1990 to 2014 and found that the area of mangroves in the region increased by about 35\%.}
elements.sent-2411-018: They believe that this is mainly due to the protection and restoration plans of the local government.
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, with the intensification of climate change and the continued advancement of coastal development in recent years, mangrove ecosystems may face new threats and opportunities, but there is a lack of systematic monitoring data.
elements.sent-2411-020: In addition, existing studies are mostly focused on individual emirates such as Abu Dhabi, and there are few comprehensive analyses of mangrove changes across the country.
elements.sent-2411-021: With its powerful feature extraction and representation capabilities, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have demonstrated superior performance in various remote sensing applications \citep{Zhu2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In semantic segmentation tasks, U-Net architecture\citep{Ronneberger2015 , SegNet \citep{Badrinarayanan2017 , and DeepLab \citep{Chen2017 are widely used in high-resolution remote sensing image analysis.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: These models can effectively integrate multi-scale features and improve segmentation accuracy through encoder-decoder structures and jump connections.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the field of mangrove monitoring with remote sensing images, deep learning methods have also been widely applied.
elements.sent-2411-025: Li et al. \citep{Li2021 used an improved U-Net model to classify mangroves in Hainan Island, China, and achieved results that were superior to traditional machine learning methods.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, the application of UNet++ in mangrove remote sensing monitoring is still very limited.
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, the potential of applying UNet++ to mangrove extraction needs further evaluation, especially in the monitoring of mangroves in arid regions like the UAE, where no relevant reports have been published yet.
elements.sent-2411-029: Specifically, the objectives of this study are: (1)Extract the spatial distribution of dense mangrove forests (coverage greater than 70\%) in the UAE and analyze their spatiotemporal trends; (2)to explore the main driving factors of mangrove changes.
elements.sent-2411-030: The major contribution of this study are: (1) Applying a deep learnig based workflow to large-scale monitoring of mangroves in the UAE based on the UNet++ model ; (2) Creating a 10-meter resolution mangrove dataset for the UAE in October 2020 using a semi-automated expert interpretation method based on the Global Mangrove Watch (GMW) v4.0 dataset combined with high-resolution satellite imagery from Google Earth; It is served as the training labels for the deep learning model.
elements.sent-2411-031: This study will provide a scientific basis for the formulation of effective mangrove protection and management strategies, which is of great significance for maintaining the health of the UAE coastal ecosystem.
elements.sent-2411-032: The UAE is located in the eastern part of the Arabian Peninsula, bordered by the Persian Gulf to the north, the Gulf of Oman to the east, and sharing borders with Saudi Arabia and Oman to the south and west, respectively, with a coastline of approximately 1,318 kilometers\citep{Abdouli2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The study area can be roughly divided into two main parts: the northern emirates (including Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm Al-Quwain, and Ras Al Khaimah, with a total area of about $2{, 622~\text{km ^2$\citep{Elmahdy2022 ) and the Emirate of Abu Dhabi.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The UAE has a tropical desert climate, characterized by hot and dry weather throughout the year, with an average annual temperature of 28°C and annual precipitation typically ranging from 50 to 100 millimeters\citep{Boer1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The main distribution areas include the eastern coastal region of Abu Dhabi, the Ras Al Khor Wildlife Sanctuary in Dubai, the Khor Kalba Nature Reserve in Sharjah, the Khor al Beidah lagoon area in Umm Al Quwain, and the coastal areas of Ras Al Khaimah.
elements.sent-2411-036: According to Alsumaiti's (2014) research, mangrove habitats in the UAE's coastal areas can be classified into three types: fringe, basin, and overwash \citep{Alsumaiti2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Avicennia marina is the primary native mangrove species in the UAE's coastal areas, capable of tolerating high soil salinity, high environmental temperatures, and limited freshwater influx \citep{Elmahdy2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The height of mangroves in the UAE ranges from a few centimeters to 3-8 meters \citep{Alsumaiti2014,Moore2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: These mangroves play a crucial role in coastal ecosystems.
elements.sent-2411-040: This study will focus on analyzing the changes in mangrove area across different emirates of the UAE, exploring various factors influencing these changes, and providing scientific basis for the conservation and management of mangroves in the UAE.
elements.sent-2411-041: The Sentinel-2 satellite was launched on June 23, 2015, equipped with a Multi-Spectral Instrument (MSI) that captures 13 spectral bands with spatial resolutions ranging from 10 to 60 meters.
elements.sent-2411-042: The visible and near-infrared bands have a resolution of 10 meters.
elements.sent-2411-043: Sentinel-2 data was chosen primarily for its high temporal resolution (5-day revisit cycle), high spatial resolution, and rich spectral information, making it particularly suitable for large-scale, long-term time series studies of mangrove forests with fine detail.
elements.sent-2411-044: All Sentinel-2 image data were acquired and pre-processed through the Google Earth Engine (GEE) platform.
elements.sent-2411-045: GEE is a cloud-based geospatial analysis platform that provides access to massive earth observation satellite data and powerful data processing capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-046: Subsequently, cloud removal was performed using the quality assessment band.
elements.sent-2411-047: To minimize the impact of tides on mangrove extraction, we used the Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI) to extract water bodies in the study area, calculated the water area for each month of every year, and then selected the two months with the smallest water area as the time window for image filtering.
elements.sent-2411-048: Images within the selected months were mosaicked and clipped to the study area extent.
elements.sent-2411-049: This method ensures that the acquired images maximally reflect the actual distribution of mangroves while minimizing the effects of tides and cloud cover.
elements.sent-2411-050: Through the GEE platform, we can quickly access and process large volumes of Sentinel-2 images, greatly improving the efficiency of data acquisition and pre-processing.
elements.sent-2411-051: This cloud computing-based approach enables us to efficiently process large amounts of spatiotemporal data, providing a reliable data foundation for subsequent mangrove change monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-052: This dataset was processed using Sentinel-2 satellite imagery with a 10-meter resolution to remap the mangrove distribution, including many areas previously unmapped.
elements.sent-2411-053: Compared to the former 25-meter pixel resolution, the new dataset’s 10-meter resolution allows for more detailed mapping of mangrove extent, capturing finer features such as fringing and riverine mangroves.
elements.sent-2411-054: As the latest and most comprehensive global mangrove distribution dataset available, the GMW dataset provides a reliable baseline for large-scale mangrove mapping.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, considering that some local inaccuracies may exist in the GMW data, this study used high-resolution satellite imagery from 2020 on the Google Earth platform to systematically refine and visually interpret mangrove distributions in the study area.
elements.sent-2411-056: The interpretation process focused on the typical characteristics of mangroves (such as canopy texture and tone) and the precise delineation of boundary areas.
elements.sent-2411-057: This dataset comprises a total of 1,568 training samples, including 346 images containing mangroves (22.07\%) and 1,222 images without mangroves (77.93\%).
elements.sent-2411-058: This approach, which combines GMW data with manual optimization based on high-resolution imagery, ensures both the systematic nature and reliability of the label data while improving the accuracy in local areas.
elements.sent-2411-059: It provides high-quality annotated data for training deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-060: The network structure is shown in Fig~\ref{fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The core innovation of this model lies in the introduction of dense skip pathways, which reduce the semantic gap between encoder and decoder through deep fusion of multi-scale features, thereby improving segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-062: In this study, UNet++ was chosen as the basic framework mainly based on the following considerations: First, mangroves often present complex boundary features and spatial heterogeneity in remote sensing images, requiring the model to have strong feature extraction and boundary recognition capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-063: Second, the dense skip connection structure helps to preserve and fuse spatial information at different scales, which is crucial for accurately identifying mangrove patches of varying sizes.
elements.sent-2411-064: For dataset partitioning, 10\% (35 images) were randomly selected from the 346 images containing mangroves to form the validation set.
elements.sent-2411-065: The remaining 311 images, along with 1222 images without mangroves, comprise the training set, totaling 1533 images.
elements.sent-2411-066: The loss function combines SoftCrossEntropyLoss and DiceLoss to address class imbalance and improve boundary segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-067: SoftCrossEntropyLoss enhances the model's generalization ability through label smoothing, while DiceLoss focuses on optimizing segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-068: The AdamW optimizer is used with an initial learning rate of 0.0001 and a weight decay of 0.001.
elements.sent-2411-069: To prevent model overfitting and improve training efficiency, a cosine annealing strategy is adopted to dynamically adjust the learning rate, with the initial restart period \( T_0 \) set to 2, the multiplier \( T_{\text{mult \) set to 2, and the minimum learning rate set to 0.00001.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The training process is set for 100 epochs, but an early stopping mechanism is introduced, halting training when the validation set's mIoU shows no improvement for 10 consecutive epochs.
elements.sent-2411-071: To enhance model robustness, data augmentation techniques are applied during training, including random flipping and rotation operations.
elements.sent-2411-072: Additionally, considering the uneven distribution of mangroves in the study area, a spatially stratified sampling strategy is adopted to construct training batches, ensuring the model can adequately learn mangrove features from different regions.
elements.sent-2411-073: Throughout the training process, the model's performance is monitored using the mIoU metric on the validation set, and the best model weights are saved for subsequent predictions.Fig~\ref{fig3 illustrates the changes in validation set mIoU and training set loss curves during the UNet++ model training process.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Throughout the entire training process, the model's loss value and validation set mIoU demonstrate a gradual convergence trend.
elements.sent-2411-075: The training loss decreases rapidly within the first 15 epochs and then stabilizes, indicating good model convergence.
elements.sent-2411-076: As training progresses, the mIoU gradually improves, ultimately reaching its best performance at the 28th epoch with an mIoU of 87.8\%.
elements.sent-2411-077: The final achievement of 87.8\% mIoU on the validation set demonstrates the effectiveness of this method in mangrove remote sensing mapping.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{fig3.png} \caption{Validation set mIoU and training set loss curves during UNet++ model training} \label{fig3}
elements.sent-2411-078: The reason for choosing these two evaluation metrics instead of overall accuracy is that the research emphasis is on the extraction effectiveness of mangroves as a specific land cover type, and non-mangrove pixels account for as high as 77.93\% of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-079: Using overall accuracy might obscure the actual performance of mangrove extraction.
elements.sent-2411-080: This data was derived from the 2020 mangrove distribution data, updated through manual visual interpretation using high-resolution Google Earth imagery to ensure the accuracy and timeliness of the test data.
elements.sent-2411-081: According to the experimental results, the model achieved excellent performance in mangrove extraction, with a producer accuracy (recall) of 0.9196, meaning the model correctly identified 91.96\% of the actual mangrove pixels.
elements.sent-2411-082: The user accuracy was 0.8917, indicating that 89.17\% of the pixels predicted as mangroves by the model were indeed mangroves.
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, the F1 score reached 0.9055, reflecting a good balance between producer accuracy and user accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-084: From the prediction result shown in Fig~\ref{fig4 , it can be visually observed that the model has a good ability to capture the boundary and morphological characteristics of mangroves.}
elements.sent-2411-085: These metrics collectively demonstrate that the UNet++ model can effectively identify and extract mangrove areas, showing strong practical value.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{fig4.png} \caption{Mangrove Extraction Results of the UNet++ Model on the Test dataset ( optical satellite image on the left, the real mangrove area map in the middle, and the predicted mangrove area map on the right )} \label{fig4}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Analysis of Mangrove Changes in the UAE (2017-2024)
elements.sent-2411-086: The results show a significant increasing trend in the total mangrove area in the UAE during this period (Fig~\ref{fig5 ), growing from 7,080.88 hectares in 2017 to 9,142.21 hectares in 2024 (Tab~\ref{tab1 ), representing a total increase of 29.11\%.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: According to research, mangroves represent the blue carbon system with the highest carbon storage potential.
elements.sent-2411-088: With the UAE's mangroves having an average carbon density of approximately 94.3 tons of carbon per hectare \citep{Carpenter2023 , it can be estimated that the carbon sequestration by UAE mangroves increased by 194,383.42 tons of carbon from 2017 to 2024.}
elements.sent-2411-089: This growth reflects the UAE's significant achievements in mangrove protection and restoration efforts.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{Figure_5.png} \caption{Changes in mangrove area in the UAE from 2017 to 2024} \label{fig5}
elements.sent-2411-090: This sustained growth trend reflects the effective measures taken by the UAE in mangrove conservation and restoration.Despite the overall growth trend, the rate of increase was not uniform.
elements.sent-2411-091: The data shows that there were several periods where the growth rate was significantly higher than the average: between 2020-2021, the area increased by 646.66 hectares, with an annual growth rate of 8.39\%, the highest within the study period; between 2023-2024, the area increased by 539.04 hectares, with an annual growth rate of 6.23\%.
elements.sent-2411-092: Growth was slow from 2017-2020, with rates below the average.
elements.sent-2411-093: In contrast, there was a slight decrease in 2021-2022, with an area reduction of 106.91 hectares, representing a 1.28\% decline.
elements.sent-2411-094: The reduction was attributed to the construction of artificial waterways, which can be directly observed from satellite imagery in Um Yifeenah Island, Abu Dhabi, demonstrating how human activities directly led to the decrease in mangrove area.
elements.sent-2411-095: Other potential factors contributing to the area reduction include climate change or natural disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-096: Notably, although the UAE's mangrove area experienced a temporary decrease, it has maintained an overall stable growth trend, reflecting the UAE's continuous efforts in protecting mangrove ecosystems.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Analysis of Changes in Mangrove Area in Each Emirate
elements.sent-2411-097: This may be due to Fujairah lacking a suitable coastal environment for mangrove growth, or not having initiated any mangrove planting projects yet.
elements.sent-2411-098: The remaining six emirates generally see an increase in mangrove area, but the patterns of change and the extent of increase vary significantly among the emirates ( Fig~\ref{fig6 ).}
elements.sent-2411-099: The following provides a detailed analysis of the changes in mangrove area from 2017 to 2024 for these six emirates.
elements.sent-2411-100: It increases from 5,530.08 hectares in 2017 to 7,385.68 hectares in 2024, an increase of 1,855.6 hectares, representing a total growth of 33.5\%.
elements.sent-2411-102: Only the period from 2021 to 2022 shows a slight decrease, with the area dropping from 6,563.75 hectares to 6,475.43 hectares.
elements.sent-2411-103: Other years maintain a significant growth trend, reaching the highest value of the observation period in 2024.
elements.sent-2411-104: The most significant growth occurs in 2017-2018, increasing by 6.44 hectares.
elements.sent-2411-105: From 2019 to 2022, it shows continuous small growth, while other years see slight decreases or remain relatively stable.
elements.sent-2411-106: It increases from 39.28 hectares in 2017 to 46.84 hectares in 2024, an increase of 7.56 hectares, representing a total growth of 19.2\%.
elements.sent-2411-107: The 2017-2018 period sees a larger increase of 4.62 hectares, and from 2021 to 2024, it shows continuous slow growth.
elements.sent-2411-108: The 2020-2021 period sees a larger decrease of 4.28 hectares.
elements.sent-2411-109: In other years, the mangrove area remains relatively stable, with annual changes around 2 hectares.
elements.sent-2411-110: It increases from 356.21 hectares in 2017 to 405.40 hectares in 2024, an increase of 49.19 hectares, representing a total growth of 13.8\%.
elements.sent-2411-111: The most significant growth occurs in 2017-2018, increasing by 33.58 hectares.
elements.sent-2411-112: From 2019 to 2022, it shows continuous growth, reaching a peak of 408.72 hectares in 2022.
elements.sent-2411-113: However, there is a relatively large decrease of 13.83 hectares in 2018-2019, and after 2022, the mangrove area remains relatively stable.
elements.sent-2411-114: It increases from 86.39 hectares in 2017 to 92.3 hectares in 2024, an increase of 5.91 hectares, representing a total growth of about 6.91\%.
elements.sent-2411-115: In 2018, 2020, and 2024, the annual growth of mangrove area is around 6 hectares, showing slow growth.
elements.sent-2411-116: In 2023, there is a larger decrease of 8.64 hectares, while other years show little change.
elements.sent-2411-117: The period from 2017 to 2024 can be roughly divided into two stages: steady growth (2017-2021) and fluctuating decline (2021-2024).
elements.sent-2411-118: From 2017 to 2021, there is a cumulative increase of 143.58 hectares, showing a significant growth, although there is a slight decrease between 2018 and 2019.
elements.sent-2411-119: After reaching a peak in 2021, it enters a stage of small fluctuations, with little overall change.
elements.sent-2411-120: From 2021 to 2024, the mangrove area changes from 1,113.67 hectares to 1,100.47 hectares.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{Figure_6.png} \caption{Mangrove area and changing trends in each emirate of the UAE} \label{fig6}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Analysis of Spatial Distribution Changes of Mangroves
elements.sent-2411-121: The blue areas represent the mangrove coverage in 2017, while the red areas represent the mangrove coverage in 2024.
elements.sent-2411-122: Numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4 indicate enlarged areas where changes are particularly noticeable.
elements.sent-2411-123: This intricate water system near Qareen Al Aish provides highly favorable conditions for mangrove growth and expansion.
elements.sent-2411-124: Between 2017 and 2024, the mangrove coverage in this area significantly increased, not only filling gaps between existing mangroves but also expanding into new suitable areas.
elements.sent-2411-125: The growth areas are often in regular rectangular shapes, indicating clear signs of artificial planting.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{fig7.png} \caption{Spatial changes in mangrove forests in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi} \label{fig7}
elements.sent-2411-126: In 2017, mangrove distribution in this area was sparse with limited coverage.
elements.sent-2411-127: By 2024, the mangrove area has significantly increased, forming a denser and more continuous vegetation belt.
elements.sent-2411-128: The curved coastline on the southeast side of the island shows the most notable mangrove expansion.
elements.sent-2411-129: The mangrove coverage around Al-Jubail Island shows the most obvious growth from 2017 to 2024.
elements.sent-2411-130: In 2017, there were only scattered mangroves around the island, mainly distributed along the southern and eastern coasts.
elements.sent-2411-131: However, by 2024, the mangrove coverage has expanded significantly, with new areas mainly concentrated in the north, west, and southwest of the island, while the original mangrove areas in the east have also further expanded.
elements.sent-2411-132: This significant growth pattern is reflected not only in the increase in area but also in the extensiveness and density of distribution.
elements.sent-2411-133: Such remarkable changes may be the result of mangrove protection and planting programs implemented in the area, effectively promoting the restoration and expansion of mangrove ecosystems.On Fahid Island, there was a small distribution of mangroves in 2017, mainly in the south and west of the island.
elements.sent-2411-134: By 2024, the mangrove area has significantly increased, especially in the west and north of the island.
elements.sent-2411-135: The distribution of mangroves around the island is more uniform and dense.Around Zeraa Island, mangroves were sparsely distributed in 2017, mainly concentrated in the north and east of the island.
elements.sent-2411-136: By 2024, mangroves have almost covered the entire periphery of the island.
elements.sent-2411-137: Particularly in the south and west of the island, a large number of new mangroves have been added.On the northeast part of Yas Island, there was some mangrove distribution in 2017.
elements.sent-2411-138: By 2024, the mangrove area has significantly increased, mainly concentrated in the northeast and east of the island.
elements.sent-2411-139: The newly added mangrove areas have clearly expanded, forming a more continuous strip-like distribution.
elements.sent-2411-140: The mangrove coverage in Jazirat Taweelah and its surrounding coastal areas has also shown a significant growth trend in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-141: The newly added areas are mainly concentrated along the eastern and southern coasts of Jazirat Taweelah Island.
elements.sent-2411-142: This significant growth pattern is reflected not only in the increase in area but also in the improvement of distribution continuity and density.
elements.sent-2411-143: The mangrove distribution is gradually spreading from the coast towards inland areas.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{fig8.png} \caption{Spatial changes in mangrove forests in the northern emirates of the UAE} \label{fig8}
elements.sent-2411-144: According to Fig~\ref{fig8 , the mangrove distribution in Dubai's Ras Al Khor Wildlife Sanctuary and its surrounding areas shows an expansion trend outward (Area 1).}
elements.sent-2411-145: In Sharjah and Ajman (Area 2), the distribution range of mangroves has also increased between 2017 and 2024.
elements.sent-2411-146: The mangrove distribution in Umm Al Quwain (Area 3) shows steady growth with incremental increases.
elements.sent-2411-147: The mangrove ecosystems in Ras Al Khaimah (Area 4 and Area 5) and Sharjah (Area 6) have also maintained a healthy state, keeping a stable growth pattern.
elements.sent-2411-148: Overall, the northern emirates have achieved positive results in the protection and development of mangrove ecosystems, laying a solid foundation for the sustainable development of coastal ecosystems.
elements.subsection-2411-010: Satellite Image Analysis of Typical Expansion Areas of Mangroves in Abu Dhabi
elements.sent-2411-149: Area a is located along the western coastal zone of Samaliyah Island.
elements.sent-2411-150: Satellite imagery from 2017 shows that this area was primarily exposed tidal flats.
elements.sent-2411-151: The 2024 satellite imagery reveals that mangroves in this area have gradually expanded into suitable zones, with a notable increase in coverage area.
elements.sent-2411-152: Area b, near the American Community School of Abu Dhabi, shows scattered mangrove distribution in 2017, but by 2024, coverage has significantly increased, forming a denser and more continuous vegetation belt.
elements.sent-2411-153: Area c, located near Zayed Military University, displays bright red mangroves and dark red salt marsh vegetation in the satellite images, with both images and interpretation results indicating a significant mangrove expansion trend.
elements.sent-2411-154: These changes reflect the improvement of Abu Dhabi's ecological environment and the effectiveness of local government protection measures.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{fig9.png} \caption{Comparison of Sentinel-2 satellite images of typical mangrove expansion areas in Abu Dhabi in 2017 and 2024} \label{fig9}
elements.subsection-2411-011: Analysis of Driving Factors for Mangrove Area Growth
elements.sent-2411-155: The Environment Agency - Abu Dhabi (EAD) has set an ambitious goal of planting 100 million mangroves by 2030 \citep{NewScientist2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: This commitment reflects the UAE's determination to address climate change and enhance the resilience of coastal ecosystems.
elements.sent-2411-157: According to reports, the project is using specially designed drones to disperse mangrove seeds, with these drones capable of scattering 2,000 seeds per minute.
elements.sent-2411-158: This efficient planting method is expected to significantly accelerate the speed of mangrove restoration.
elements.sent-2411-159: Furthermore, the project utilizes artificial intelligence technology to identify areas most suitable for mangrove growth, thereby improving the success rate of planting.
elements.sent-2411-160: The "Blue Carbon" project, launched by the UAE government in 2013, marks an important milestone in the country's recognition and utilization of the carbon sequestration capacity of marine ecosystems\citep{UAEBGC2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-161: This project not only demonstrates the government's profound understanding of the ecological value of mangroves but has also directly promoted mangrove area growth in several key regions.
elements.sent-2411-162: The implementation of the "Blue Carbon" project has brought particularly significant results in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi.
elements.sent-2411-163: A decree issued in 2020 prohibits damage to mangroves, with violators facing fines of up to 10,000 dirhams, providing strong legal protection \citep{TheNational2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-164: These policy measures have provided robust institutional support for the growth of mangrove areas, which may explain the sustained growth trend observed in this study, especially in the Abu Dhabi region.
elements.sent-2411-165: The developed root systems of mangroves can survive in high-salinity environments, and their special leaf structures can reduce water evaporation \citep{ReefResilience2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-166: They have strong resistance to strong winds and storm surges, enabling them to adapt to the frequent sandstorms in Abu Dhabi.
elements.sent-2411-167: The latest research by Alothman et al. (2023) found that mangroves in the Arabian Gulf, especially Avicennia marina, have developed unique physiological adaptations that allow them to thrive in environments of extreme heat and high salinity, enabling them to survive and expand in the UAE's harsh climate conditions \citep{Alothman2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: This adaptability is particularly important against the backdrop of rising temperatures and sea levels in the region.
elements.sent-2411-169: Through nested skip connections and deep feature fusion, UNet++ successfully addressed the complex boundary issues of mangroves and significantly improved segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-170: The validation set results of this study show that the UNet++ model achieved an mIoU of 87.8\% in mangrove extraction.
elements.sent-2411-171: Between 2017-2024, dense mangrove areas increased by approximately 2,061.33 hectares, equivalent to sequestering 194,383.42 tons of carbon.
elements.sent-2411-172: Abu Dhabi, being the largest emirate in the UAE and the main region for mangrove distribution, has made the primary contribution to mangrove growth.
elements.sent-2411-173: The large-scale mangrove planting projects implemented in Abu Dhabi in recent years have directly promoted the expansion of UAE's mangroves.
elements.sent-2411-174: Areas such as Qareen Al Aish, Al Hudayriat Island, and Al-Jubail Island in Abu Dhabi have shown evident patterns of mangrove expansion, with notably increased mangrove density, especially within protected areas and vegetation restoration zones.
elements.sent-2411-175: Other emirates have also shown stable growth trends and have made efforts in mangrove protection and restoration.
elements.sent-2411-176: The UAE has achieved remarkable results in mangrove restoration projects, setting an example for global mangrove ecosystem restoration.
elements.sent-2411-177: The research findings not only provide scientific basis for developing more targeted mangrove protection and restoration policies, but also offer effective reference methods for remote sensing monitoring of mangroves in other arid climate regions, contributing to the promotion of global mangrove ecosystem protection and management.
elements.sent-2411-178: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests.. \printendnotes.
elements.sent-2411-179: Results show that the total mangrove area in the UAE in 2024 was approximately 9,142.21 hectares, an increase of 2,061.33 hectares compared to 2017, with carbon sequestration increasing by approximately 194,383.42 tons, equivalent to fixing about 713,367.36 tons of carbon dioxide.
elements.sent-2411-180: With the UAE's mangroves having an average carbon density of approximately 94.3 tons of carbon per hectare \citep{Carpenter2023 , it can be estimated that the carbon sequestration by UAE mangroves increased by 194,383.42 tons of carbon from 2017 to 2024, equivalent to fixing about 713,367.36 tons of carbon dioxide.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Between 2017-2024, dense mangrove areas increased by approximately 2,061.33 hectares, equivalent to sequestering 194,383.42 tons of carbon, fixing about 713,367.36 tons of carbon dioxide.
